frontmatter.title: Reduced Formulation for Elliptic Optimal Control

children.value:
This is the first genuinely PDE-constrained lecture in the course. We take the
optimization viewpoint of Lecture 3 and apply it to a standard linear elliptic
optimal control problem:
the PDE defines a control-to-state map

children.html: SSS

children.value: the cost becomes a reduced functional

children.html: f(u)=J(Su,u)f(u)=J(Su,u)f(u)=J(Su,u)

children.value: the adjoint equation gives the reduced gradient at the cost of one extra PDE solve;
gradient-based methods from Lecture 3 now become PDE-based algorithms.

We focus on the distributed-control Poisson model because it contains all the
structural ideas we need before constraints, discretization, and more advanced PDEs.

children.html: Ω⊂Rd\Omega\subset\mathbb R^dΩ⊂Rd

children.value:
be a bounded domain.
We consider the distributed control problem

This is the PDE analogue of the linear-quadratic finite-dimensional problems seen before.
Weak Formulation of the State Equation

children.label: Weak Formulation of the State Equation

children.value:
This is the first point where the PDE language changes.
We start from the strong form

children.html: {−Δy=uin Ω,y=0on ∂Ω.\begin{cases} -\Delta y = u & \text{in }\Omega,\\ y = 0 & \text{on }\partial\Omega. \end{cases}{−Δy=uy=0​in Ω,on ∂Ω.​

Take a test function

children.value: This allows us to define the state equation as a linear map

children.html: S:L2(Ω)→H01(Ω),u↦y.S:L^2(\Omega)\to H_0^1(\Omega),\qquad u\mapsto y.S:L2(Ω)→H01​(Ω),u↦y.

children.value: The distinction to keep in mind is:
strong formulation: pointwise PDE, more regularity required;
weak formulation: variational identity, Sobolev-space setting.

With elliptic regularity one often has more, but for the reduced formulation
the key point is simply:

children.html: uuu
y=S(u)y=S(u)y=S(u)

children.value: the control is the only independent optimization variable.
Eliminate the state through the PDE:

children.html: y=S(u).y=S(u).y=S(u).
f(u):=J(S(u),u)=12∥S(u)−yd∥L2(Ω)2+α2∥u∥L2(Ω)2.f(u):=J(S(u),u) = \frac12\|S(u)-y_d\|_{L^2(\Omega)}^2 +\frac{\alpha}{2}\|u\|_{L^2(\Omega)}^2.f(u):=J(S(u),u)=21​∥S(u)−yd​∥L2(Ω)2​+2α​∥u∥L2(Ω)2​.

children.value:
It is also useful to introduce the same problem in a fully operatorial form, because
this makes the infinite-dimensional structure look exactly like a block linear system.

children.value: express the PDE constraint and its adjoint coupling;

children.html: (3,3)(3,3)(3,3)

children.value: position is the characteristic signature of a saddle-point problem.

So even before discretization, PDE-constrained optimization already has the same algebraic
structure as the block KKT systems that appear in finite-dimensional constrained optimization.

In finite dimensions, existence follows from the Weierstrass principle:
closed and bounded sets are compact, so minimizing sequences have convergent subsequences.
That argument is no longer available in infinite-dimensional spaces, because
closed and bounded sets in

children.html: L2(Ω)L^2(\Omega)L2(Ω)

children.value: So, in addition to convexity, we need three structural ingredients:
coercivity, to prevent minimizing sequences from escaping to infinity;
weak compactness of bounded sets, which is available in reflexive spaces such as

children.html: L2(Ω)L^2(\Omega)L2(Ω)

children.value: weak lower semicontinuity, so the limit of a minimizing sequence still minimizes the functional.
For our reduced functional, coercivity comes from the Tikhonov term:

children.html: f(u)=12∥S(u)−yd∥L2(Ω)2+α2∥u∥L2(Ω)2≥α2∥u∥L2(Ω)2.f(u) = \frac12\|S(u)-y_d\|_{L^2(\Omega)}^2 +\frac{\alpha}{2}\|u\|_{L^2(\Omega)}^2 \ge \frac{\alpha}{2}\|u\|_{L^2(\Omega)}^2.f(u)=21​∥S(u)−yd​∥L2(Ω)2​+2α​∥u∥L2(Ω)2​≥2α​∥u∥L2(Ω)2​.

children.value: Hence every minimizing sequence is bounded in

children.html: L2(Ω)L^2(\Omega)L2(Ω)

children.value: To see this more concretely, let

children.html: (un)(u_n)(un​)

children.value:
be a minimizing sequence.
Then there exists a constant

children.html: CCC

children.value:
f(u_n)\le C
\qquad \text{for all } n \text{ large enough}.
children.html: f(un)≤Cfor all n large enough.f(u_n)\le C \qquad \text{for all } n \text{ large enough}.f(un​)≤Cfor all n large enough.

α2∥un∥L2(Ω)2≤f(un)≤C⟹∥un∥L2(Ω)≤R=2Cα\frac{\alpha}{2}\|u_n\|_{L^2(\Omega)}^2 \le f(u_n) \le C \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \|u_n\|_{L^2(\Omega)} \le R = \sqrt{\frac{2C}{\alpha}}2α​∥un​∥L2(Ω)2​≤f(un​)≤C⟹∥un​∥L2(Ω)​≤R=α2C​​

children.value: so the minimizing sequence is bounded.

children.html: L2(Ω)L^2(\Omega)L2(Ω)

children.value: is reflexive, bounded sequences admit weakly convergent subsequences:

children.html: un⇀uˉin L2(Ω).u_n \rightharpoonup \bar u \qquad \text{in } L^2(\Omega).un​⇀uˉin L2(Ω).

children.value:
For minimization we do not need strong convergence of the whole sequence:
we only need one convergent subsequence and a notion of lower semicontinuity that is compatible with that convergence.
If

children.html: fff
f(uˉ)≤lim inf⁡n→∞f(un).f(\bar u)\le \liminf_{n\to\infty} f(u_n).f(uˉ)≤n→∞liminf​f(un​).
(un)(u_n)(un​)

children.value: is minimizing, the right-hand side is exactly

children.html: inf⁡f\inf finff
f(uˉ)≤inf⁡f,f(\bar u)\le \inf f,f(uˉ)≤inff,
f(uˉ)=inf⁡ff(\bar u)=\inf ff(uˉ)=inff

children.value:
.
So the weak limit of a minimizing subsequence is already a minimizer.

The state equation is linear and continuous, so the reduced functional is convex and weakly lower semicontinuous.
This allows us to pass to the limit along a minimizing sequence and obtain existence of a minimizer.

Uniqueness comes from strict convexity.
The reduced functional

children.html: fff
SSS
α2∥u∥2\frac{\alpha}{2}\|u\|^22α​∥u∥2
α>0\alpha>0α>0

uˉ∈L2(Ω)\bar u\in L^2(\Omega)uˉ∈L2(Ω)

children.value: This is the first major structural simplification of the linear-quadratic elliptic case:
existence is obtained through coercivity + weak compactness + weak lower semicontinuity;
first-order optimality will characterize the global minimizer.
Directional Derivative of the Reduced Cost

children.value:
This formula is correct, but not computationally convenient:
if we need to evaluate

children.html: f′(u)hf'(u)hf′(u)h
hhh

children.value: , we would need many state solves.
The adjoint equation removes this difficulty.
At this point we know that

children.html: f′(u)h=(y−yd,S(h))L2(Ω)+α(u,h)L2(Ω).f'(u)h=(y-y_d,S(h))_{L^2(\Omega)}+\alpha(u,h)_{L^2(\Omega)}.f′(u)h=(y−yd​,S(h))L2(Ω)​+α(u,h)L2(Ω)​.

children.value:
The difficulty is the first term:
the direction

children.html: hhh

children.value: appears only indirectly, through the state variation

children.html: S(h)S(h)S(h)

children.value:
This is exactly where the adjoint enters.
We would like to rewrite

children.html: (y−yd,S(h))L2(Ω)⟹(S∗(y−yd),h)L2(Ω),(y-y_d,S(h))_{L^2(\Omega)} \qquad \Longrightarrow\qquad (S^*(y-y_d),h)_{L^2(\Omega)},(y−yd​,S(h))L2(Ω)​⟹(S∗(y−yd​),h)L2(Ω)​,
hhh

children.value:
appears explicitly.
This can be done in a two-step process. Given a control

children.html: uuu

children.value: , we first solve the control-to-state map to get

children.html: y=S(u)y=S(u)y=S(u)

children.value: , then we solve the one adjoint PDE to obtain

children.html: p=S∗(y−yd)p=S^*(y-y_d)p=S∗(y−yd​)
ppp

children.value: to rewrite the inner product with

children.html: S(h)S(h)S(h)
hhh

such that
for every test function

children.html: v∈H01(Ω)v\in H_0^1(\Omega)v∈H01​(Ω)
∫Ω∇v⋅∇p dx=∫Ω(y−yd)v dx.\int_\Omega \nabla v\cdot \nabla p\,dx = \int_\Omega (y-y_d)v\,dx.∫Ω​∇v⋅∇pdx=∫Ω​(y−yd​)vdx.

children.value: Notice the change of roles of

children.html: vvv
ppp

children.value:
compared to the state equation. In
this particular example the bilinear form is symmetric, so the change of roles
is not visible, but in general the adjoint bilinear form is different from the
original one, and the test function

children.html: vvv

children.value: is now associated with the adjoint state

children.html: ppp

children.value: Notice the analogy with the state equation:
the left-hand side is the adjoint bilinear form;
the source term is now the tracking residual
